text: Running speed during training sessions is the strongest predictor of marathon race time in recreational male marathoners, explaining 40% of variance alone.
Percent body fat is the second strongest predictor of marathon race time after training speed, with a standardized coefficient of β = 0.27.
Marathon race time in recreational male runners can be estimated as: race time (minutes) = 326.3 + 2.394 × (percent body fat, %) - 12.06 × (training speed, km/h), explaining 44% of variance.
Training speed is more predictive of marathon performance than anthropometric characteristics, accounting for 40% vs 4% additional variance from body composition.
Running speed during training sessions is correlated with pre-race percent body fat, suggesting leaner runners tend to train faster.
Recreational male marathoners who train at speeds close to approximately 11 km/h (~8:45 min/mile) tend to achieve faster marathon race times.
